cards.title: POCUS — Introduction & Probe Selection

alerts.label: Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS)
alerts.text: POCUS is the use of ultrasound at the bedside to answer specific clinical questions in real time. It is NOT a comprehensive imaging study — it is a focused diagnostic tool that extends the physical examination. Core POCUS applications: FAST (trauma), cardiac, lung, IVC, abdominal aorta, renal, DVT, soft tissue. BNYS practitioners with POCUS training can rapidly triage patients, reduce unnecessary referrals, and make more confident clinical decisions.

rows: Abdominal organs, FAST, aorta, renal, OB, IVC
DVT, soft tissue, MSK, thyroid, vascular access, lung (pleural line)
Cardiac POCUS, IVC, pericardial effusion — small footprint for rib spaces
Transvaginal OB, prostate, pelvic — specialist use

pearls.label: 🟡 POCUS Knobology — Essential Controls
pearls.text: Depth: increase to see deeper structures; decrease to zoom in on superficial structures. Gain: overall brightness — increase if image too dark; decrease if too bright (gain ≠ depth). Focus: position at the structure of interest for maximum resolution. Frequency: higher frequency = better resolution, less depth. Most modern machines auto-optimise — but knowing these saves time. Orientation marker: probe marker (ridge/light) corresponds to left side of screen by convention in abdominal/vascular scans; to patient's right in cardiac.

cards._legacyHtml:
Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS)
POCUS is the use of ultrasound at the bedside to answer specific clinical questions in real time. It is NOT a comprehensive imaging study — it is a focused diagnostic tool that extends the physical examination. Core POCUS applications: FAST (trauma), cardiac, lung, IVC, abdominal aorta, renal, DVT, soft tissue. BNYS practitioners with POCUS training can rapidly triage patients, reduce unnecessary referrals, and make more confident clinical decisions.
ProbeFrequencyDepthApplicationsImage Shape
Curvilinear (convex)2–5 MHzDeep (15–30cm)Abdominal organs, FAST, aorta, renal, OB, IVCWide sector (fan-shaped)
Linear (high-frequency)7–15 MHzSuperficial (<6cm)DVT, soft tissue, MSK, thyroid, vascular access, lung (pleural line)Rectangular
Phased array (cardiac)1–5 MHzDeep (20cm)Cardiac POCUS, IVC, pericardial effusion — small footprint for rib spacesNarrow sector fan
Endocavitary5–9 MHzModerateTransvaginal OB, prostate, pelvic — specialist useMicroconvex
🟡 POCUS Knobology — Essential Controls
Depth: increase to see deeper structures; decrease to zoom in on superficial structures. Gain: overall brightness — increase if image too dark; decrease if too bright (gain ≠ depth). Focus: position at the structure of interest for maximum resolution. Frequency: higher frequency = better resolution, less depth. Most modern machines auto-optimise — but knowing these saves time. Orientation marker: probe marker (ridge/light) corresponds to left side of screen by convention in abdominal/vascular scans; to patient's right in cardiac.

cards.title: FAST Exam — Focused Assessment with Sonography in Trauma

alerts.label: FAST Exam — 4 Windows in <3 Minutes
alerts.text: FAST detects free intraperitoneal fluid (blood) in trauma. Sensitivity 73–99% for haemoperitoneum requiring surgery. A positive FAST in an unstable patient = emergency surgical consultation immediately. A negative FAST does NOT exclude injury — especially solid organ injury without haemoperitoneum.

rows: Emergency surgical consultation IMMEDIATELY. Activate massive transfusion protocol.
Free fluid present — may be minor or significant
CT abdomen + pelvis with contrast for definitive assessment. Serial FAST monitoring.
Does not exclude injury — solid organ injuries often FAST-negative
CT if clinically indicated. Serial FAST (repeat at 30 min). Do not clear patient based on FAST alone.

steps.title: RUQ Window (Morrison's Pouch) — Most Sensitive
steps.text: Probe: curvilinear. Patient supine. Probe in right mid-axillary line, 8th–10th rib space, marker cephalad. View: liver-kidney interface (Morrison's pouch). Positive: anechoic (black) stripe between liver and right kidney >5mm. Also look: subcapsular haematoma (crescentic hypoechoic region at liver edge), liver lacerations (irregular hyperechoic areas).

steps.title: LUQ Window (Splenorenal Recess)
steps.text: Probe: curvilinear. Left posterior axillary line, 8th–10th rib space, marker cephalad. View: spleen-kidney interface and splenorenal recess. Note: LUQ is harder to image due to rib shadowing — angle posteriorly. Positive: anechoic stripe between spleen and left kidney. Also: perisplenic fluid, subphrenic fluid (between spleen and diaphragm).

steps.title: Subxiphoid Window — Cardiac
steps.text: Probe: curvilinear or phased array. Subxiphoid, probe flat against abdomen, marker to patient's right. View: 4-chamber cardiac view. Look for: pericardial effusion (anechoic stripe around heart >2mm), RV collapse (tamponade — diastolic RV free wall collapse), grossly reduced LV function. Positive for tamponade: effusion + RV collapse = surgical emergency.

steps.title: Pelvic Window (Pouch of Douglas)
steps.text: Probe: curvilinear. Suprapubic, probe transverse then longitudinal. View: bladder as acoustic window to posterior pelvis. Look for: anechoic fluid posterior to bladder (in males = rectovesical pouch; in females = rectouterine pouch of Douglas — most dependent position in supine). Positive: fluid stripe posterior to or surrounding bladder. Beware: ovarian cysts can mimic free fluid in women.

cards._legacyHtml:
FAST Exam — 4 Windows in <3 Minutes
FAST detects free intraperitoneal fluid (blood) in trauma. Sensitivity 73–99% for haemoperitoneum requiring surgery. A positive FAST in an unstable patient = emergency surgical consultation immediately. A negative FAST does NOT exclude injury — especially solid organ injury without haemoperitoneum.
1
RUQ Window (Morrison's Pouch) — Most Sensitive Probe: curvilinear. Patient supine. Probe in right mid-axillary line, 8th–10th rib space, marker cephalad. View: liver-kidney interface (Morrison's pouch). Positive: anechoic (black) stripe between liver and right kidney >5mm. Also look: subcapsular haematoma (crescentic hypoechoic region at liver edge), liver lacerations (irregular hyperechoic areas).
2
LUQ Window (Splenorenal Recess) Probe: curvilinear. Left posterior axillary line, 8th–10th rib space, marker cephalad. View: spleen-kidney interface and splenorenal recess. Note: LUQ is harder to image due to rib shadowing — angle posteriorly. Positive: anechoic stripe between spleen and left kidney. Also: perisplenic fluid, subphrenic fluid (between spleen and diaphragm).
3
Subxiphoid Window — Cardiac Probe: curvilinear or phased array. Subxiphoid, probe flat against abdomen, marker to patient's right. View: 4-chamber cardiac view. Look for: pericardial effusion (anechoic stripe around heart >2mm), RV collapse (tamponade — diastolic RV free wall collapse), grossly reduced LV function. Positive for tamponade: effusion + RV collapse = surgical emergency.
4
Pelvic Window (Pouch of Douglas) Probe: curvilinear. Suprapubic, probe transverse then longitudinal. View: bladder as acoustic window to posterior pelvis. Look for: anechoic fluid posterior to bladder (in males = rectovesical pouch; in females = rectouterine pouch of Douglas — most dependent position in supine). Positive: fluid stripe posterior to or surrounding bladder. Beware: ovarian cysts can mimic free fluid in women.
FindingInterpretationAction
Positive FAST + haemodynamically unstableSignificant haemoperitoneum — likely surgical haemorrhageEmergency surgical consultation IMMEDIATELY. Activate massive transfusion protocol.
Positive FAST + haemodynamically stableFree fluid present — may be minor or significantCT abdomen + pelvis with contrast for definitive assessment. Serial FAST monitoring.
Negative FAST + high clinical suspicionDoes not exclude injury — solid organ injuries often FAST-negativeCT if clinically indicated. Serial FAST (repeat at 30 min). Do not clear patient based on FAST alone.
Pericardial effusion + RV collapseCardiac tamponadeEmergency pericardiocentesis. Cardiothoracic surgical consultation.

cards.title: Lung POCUS — Pneumothorax, Effusion & Pulmonary Oedema

alerts.label: Lung POCUS — Key Artefacts as Diagnostic Signs
alerts.text: Lung POCUS interprets acoustic artefacts caused by air and fluid interfaces — not direct tissue imaging. Each artefact has specific diagnostic meaning. Normal lung: A-lines + lung sliding. Pathological lung: specific patterns indicate pneumothorax, effusion, or consolidation.

rows: Shimmering movement of pleural line with respiration (like ants marching)
Normal — visceral and parietal pleura in contact and moving
Normal lung. ABSENT in: pneumothorax, mainstem intubation, pleurodesis, apnoea
Horizontal hyperechoic reverberation artefacts below pleural line, equidistant repeating
Air below pleural line — normal lung or pneumothorax
Normal lung (with sliding) OR pneumothorax (without sliding)
Vertical hyperechoic lines arising from pleural line, extend to screen edge, erase A-lines, move with lung
≥3 B-lines per zone = significant interstitial syndrome. Pulmonary oedema (bilateral), pneumonia, ARDS, interstitial lung disease
Transition between absent and present lung sliding at a single point — pathognomonic
Pneumothorax margin — where collapsed lung meets chest wall
Pneumothorax ★★★ — 100% specific (if found, pneumothorax confirmed). Sensitivity lower than absence of sliding.
Anechoic space above diaphragm. Sinusoid pattern on M-mode (fluid moving with respiration)
Effusion ★★★. POCUS can detect as little as 20ml. Allows guided thoracentesis.
Lung tissue appears liver-like (solid) with dynamic air bronchograms
Pneumonia ★★★ (most common), atelectasis (static air bronchograms), haemorrhage, tumour

cards._legacyHtml:
Lung POCUS — Key Artefacts as Diagnostic Signs
Lung POCUS interprets acoustic artefacts caused by air and fluid interfaces — not direct tissue imaging. Each artefact has specific diagnostic meaning. Normal lung: A-lines + lung sliding. Pathological lung: specific patterns indicate pneumothorax, effusion, or consolidation.
POCUS SignAppearanceMeaningConditions
Lung SlidingShimmering movement of pleural line with respiration (like ants marching)Normal — visceral and parietal pleura in contact and movingNormal lung. ABSENT in: pneumothorax, mainstem intubation, pleurodesis, apnoea
A-LinesHorizontal hyperechoic reverberation artefacts below pleural line, equidistant repeatingAir below pleural line — normal lung or pneumothoraxNormal lung (with sliding) OR pneumothorax (without sliding)
B-Lines (Comet Tails)Vertical hyperechoic lines arising from pleural line, extend to screen edge, erase A-lines, move with lungThickened interlobular septa — interstitial fluid≥3 B-lines per zone = significant interstitial syndrome. Pulmonary oedema (bilateral), pneumonia, ARDS, interstitial lung disease
Lung PointTransition between absent and present lung sliding at a single point — pathognomonicPneumothorax margin — where collapsed lung meets chest wallPneumothorax ★★★ — 100% specific (if found, pneumothorax confirmed). Sensitivity lower than absence of sliding.
Quad Sign / Sinusoid SignAnechoic space above diaphragm. Sinusoid pattern on M-mode (fluid moving with respiration)Pleural effusionEffusion ★★★. POCUS can detect as little as 20ml. Allows guided thoracentesis.
Tissue-like pattern (hepatisation)Lung tissue appears liver-like (solid) with dynamic air bronchogramsConsolidation — air replaced by fluid/pus/tissuePneumonia ★★★ (most common), atelectasis (static air bronchograms), haemorrhage, tumour

cards.title: IVC Collapsibility & Cardiac POCUS

rows: Hypovolaemia likely — fluid responsive. Consider IV fluids if clinically indicated.
Normal to low CVP. Clinical context determines management.
Consider heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, pericardial disease.
Right heart failure, tamponade, tension pneumothorax, severe pulmonary hypertension. Fluid CAUTIOUSLY.

pearls.label: 🟡 Pericardial vs Pleural Effusion Differentiation
pearls.text: Critical distinction: pericardial effusion lies ANTERIOR to descending aorta (posterior cardiac structure). Pleural effusion lies POSTERIOR to descending aorta. On PLAX view: if anechoic collection is anterior to the descending aorta → pericardial effusion. If posterior → pleural effusion. This distinction prevents misdiagnosis of left pleural effusion as pericardial effusion — a common error that leads to inappropriate pericardiocentesis.

steps.title: Basic Cardiac POCUS — Parasternal Long Axis (PLAX)
steps.text: Probe: phased array. Position: left parasternal, 3rd–4th ICS. Marker: patient's RIGHT shoulder. View: left ventricle, mitral valve, aortic valve, left atrium, descending aorta (posterior). Assess: LV size (dilated = DCM), LV function (EF estimation — "eyeball EF"), pericardial effusion (anechoic stripe posterior to heart — posterior effusion seen first), aortic root size (>4cm = consider aortic aneurysm).

steps.title: Apical 4-Chamber (A4C)
steps.text: Probe: phased array. Position: cardiac apex (point of maximal impulse). Marker: patient's LEFT. View: all 4 chambers simultaneously. Assess: relative chamber sizes (RV should be smaller than LV — RV dilation = cor pulmonale, PE, RV failure), septal motion (paradoxical in PE, RV overload), LV regional wall motion abnormalities (RWMA — flat segment = ischaemia/MI). Mitral and tricuspid valve leaflet motion (coaptation).

cards._legacyHtml:
IVC Size / CollapsibilityEstimated CVPVolume StatusClinical Interpretation
<2.1cm + >50% collapse with sniff0–5 mmHgVolume depletedHypovolaemia likely — fluid responsive. Consider IV fluids if clinically indicated.
2.1cm + >50% collapse0–10 mmHgEuvolaemic or mildly lowNormal to low CVP. Clinical context determines management.
2.1cm + <50% collapse10–20 mmHgEuvolaemic to slightly elevatedConsider heart failure, pulmonary hypertension, pericardial disease.
>2.1cm + <50% collapse (plethoric IVC)>15–20 mmHgVolume overloaded / raised RA pressureRight heart failure, tamponade, tension pneumothorax, severe pulmonary hypertension. Fluid CAUTIOUSLY.
1
Basic Cardiac POCUS — Parasternal Long Axis (PLAX) Probe: phased array. Position: left parasternal, 3rd–4th ICS. Marker: patient's RIGHT shoulder. View: left ventricle, mitral valve, aortic valve, left atrium, descending aorta (posterior). Assess: LV size (dilated = DCM), LV function (EF estimation — "eyeball EF"), pericardial effusion (anechoic stripe posterior to heart — posterior effusion seen first), aortic root size (>4cm = consider aortic aneurysm).
2
Apical 4-Chamber (A4C) Probe: phased array. Position: cardiac apex (point of maximal impulse). Marker: patient's LEFT. View: all 4 chambers simultaneously. Assess: relative chamber sizes (RV should be smaller than LV — RV dilation = cor pulmonale, PE, RV failure), septal motion (paradoxical in PE, RV overload), LV regional wall motion abnormalities (RWMA — flat segment = ischaemia/MI). Mitral and tricuspid valve leaflet motion (coaptation).
🟡 Pericardial vs Pleural Effusion Differentiation
Critical distinction: pericardial effusion lies ANTERIOR to descending aorta (posterior cardiac structure). Pleural effusion lies POSTERIOR to descending aorta. On PLAX view: if anechoic collection is anterior to the descending aorta → pericardial effusion. If posterior → pleural effusion. This distinction prevents misdiagnosis of left pleural effusion as pericardial effusion — a common error that leads to inappropriate pericardiocentesis.
